q: Which of these causes the phenomenon of day and night on Earth?
sol: The Earth rotates on its axis once every 24 hours, causing different parts to face the Sun.

q: The Moon completes one revolution around the Earth in approximately how many days?
sol: It takes about 27.3 days for the Moon to orbit the Earth, which is roughly a month.

q: What is the imaginary line on which the Earth rotates called?
sol: The axis is an imaginary line passing through the North and South poles.

q: A solar eclipse occurs when ________ comes between the Sun and the Earth.
sol: During a solar eclipse, the Moon blocks the sunlight from reaching parts of the Earth.

q: Which planet is known as the 'Blue Planet'?
sol: Earth is called the Blue Planet because nearly 71% of its surface is covered by water.

q: The changing shapes of the visible part of the Moon are called:
sol: Phases occur because we see different amounts of the Moon's illuminated side as it orbits Earth.

q: What is the primary cause of seasons on Earth?
sol: The 23.5-degree tilt of Earth's axis as it revolves around the Sun creates seasonal changes.

q: The Earth's path around the Sun is called its:
sol: An orbit is the fixed path followed by a celestial body under the influence of gravity.

q: Which of these is the largest object in our solar system?
sol: The Sun contains more than 99% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

q: When the Moon is between the Earth and the Sun, we see a:
sol: At New Moon, the side of the Moon facing Earth is dark because it is not receiving direct sunlight.

q: What is the force that keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?
sol: The Sun's massive gravitational pull keeps all planets, including Earth, in their orbits.

q: A leap year has 366 days because Earth takes ________ to orbit the Sun.
sol: The extra quarter day adds up to one full day every four years.

q: The dark, flat areas on the Moon's surface are called:
sol: Early astronomers thought these dark volcanic plains were actual seas of water.

q: Which layer of the Earth do we live on?
sol: The crust is the thin, outermost rocky shell of the Earth.

q: A lunar eclipse can only happen during which moon phase?
sol: A lunar eclipse occurs when Earth is directly between the Sun and a Full Moon.

q: What protects the Earth from harmful solar radiation?
sol: The atmosphere, specifically the ozone layer, filters out dangerous UV rays.

q: The time taken for one complete revolution of Earth is:
sol: One revolution around the Sun defines a solar year.

q: Tides in the Earth's oceans are primarily caused by:
sol: The Moon's gravitational pull creates a 'bulge' in the oceans, leading to high and low tides.

q: The Sun is actually a:
sol: The Sun is a medium-sized star made of hot, glowing gases.

q: What are the bowl-shaped holes on the Moon's surface called?
sol: Craters are formed by the impact of meteoroids hitting the Moon's surface.

q: Which part of the Earth receives direct sunlight all year round?
sol: The Equator is the middle belt of the Earth and receives consistent solar energy.

q: How many planets are there in our solar system?
sol: There are eight recognized planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

q: The Sun rises in the East and sets in the West because Earth rotates from:
sol: The counter-clockwise (West to East) rotation makes the Sun appear to move across the sky.

q: The outermost part of the Sun's atmosphere seen during a total eclipse is the:
sol: The Corona appears as a pearly white glow around the dark silhouette of the Moon.

q: There is no sound on the Moon because it lacks:
sol: Sound needs a medium like air to travel; without an atmosphere, the Moon is silent.
